Looking at the function, the key logic is on line 64:

```python
if now > adjusted_expiry:
    return None
```

The docstring says "The entry is returned only when the current time has not yet reached the expiration time." This means the entry should be returned when `now < expires_at` (strictly less than). The current condition returns `None` when `now > adjusted_expiry`, which means when `now == adjusted_expiry`, it returns the value. 

However, the more likely bug is the comparison operator. The condition should use `>=` instead of `>` to properly handle the case where the current time equals the expiration time (the entry has expired). When `now == adjusted_expiry`, the entry has technically expired (the current time has "reached" the expiration time), but the current code returns the value instead of `None`.

This is a boundary condition bug - the entry should be considered expired when `now >= adjusted_expiry`, not just when `now > adjusted_expiry`.
